data.title: Why Pfizer's Headwinds May Already Be In The Price
data.description:
Pfizer (NYSE:PFE) faces several headwinds ranging from patent expirations to COVID product declines and competition from generics. Some of these factors have mostly played out, like the COVID product cliff, while the others are already acknowledged in today's valuations. That is my Buy thesis. My cautious Buy is not rooted in defense of the headwinds - they are real. I argue how the "most likely scenario" is priced in. Fresh entries will therefore not only likely see a sustained dividend, but capital appreciation due to even partial pipeline and acquisition successes.

Acknowledging the Headwinds

Within the 2026-28 window, 5 of Pfizer's top franchises face loss of exclusivity. In my scenario estimates, I have assumed conservative revenue trajectories of the existing products. Eliquis, facing a patent cliff in 2028, goes through an 8% growth (in line with what we saw in Q2 2025) before an 85% sharp drop due to competition and further drops by 5% every year. That is a whopping ~$8.5b revenue loss from the peak.

The Prevnar family, facing an immediate patent cliff in 2026, is also assumed to see revenues drop by 85% followed by sequential annual drops of 5%. This assumes no settlement scenarios that could limit losses. Similarly Ibrance and Xtandi run into cliffs in 2027 and account for ~$5b revenue drop from peak values. Xeljanz is expiring in 2028 but has a relatively lower impact than the other products at stake.

Taken together, over the next 2-3 years, I believe patent cliffs alone could account for ~$19-20b revenue erosion. There are additional patent expiry impacts on "Other Oncology" too, with varying expiries between 2028-2030 (modeled as a 40% decline in year 1 followed by a 10% decline thereafter). "Other Products" also face competitive erosion due to biosimilar or generic entry in the 2027-29 window. I have taken an overall 45% drop in the first year, followed by 10% drops in subsequent years, acknowledging the varied profile of products here. Some products, like adult Prevnar, should see sharper declines, while smaller injectables should hold up better.

There are impacts of slowing Paxlovid revenues, which I have assumed to decay 2% annually from here on (most of the impact is already in, as seen in Q2 2025 revenues of $450m only). My model also extrapolates a 2% decline in seasonal vaccine demand in Comirnaty.

Since my patent cliff scenarios are aggressive, I have not separately modeled for other risks that exist like the Medicare Part D Rebate Clawbacks that reduce net pricing room for key specialty drugs. The extent of impact there is ~$1b and should already be impacting 2025 revenues.

Plugging the Hole

From the existing product basket, only three products stand out as growth possibilities that can offset some of the existing product erosion. Nurtec is currently growing at ~10% and I have assumed a 8% continued CAGR growth on its lower base. Padcev, a high-growth ADC in bladder/multiple myeloma, grew by ~60% YoY in Q2 2025. I have assumed ~25% CAGR growth here. I also see a continued ~25% CAGR growth in the Vyndaqel Family for its growing use in transthyretin amyloidosis.

Put together, the growth of some existing products still shrinks revenues to ~$45b by 2030. These are aggressive topline decline scenarios for expiring drugs factored in, while the assumed growth of existing products is not out of line - an extrapolation or even more moderate growth rates than current growth have been assumed.

The current valuations of ~8x is lower than the last 5 year average valuations of ~10x earnings (and far below the sector median of ~19x). These valuations bake in a ~20% EPS erosion and revenue of ~54.5b by 2030. Those levels of revenues are still ~$10b higher than the base case scenario I have modeled that factors in aggressive patent risks and viable existing portfolio growth.

With a budget for ~$10b-$15b for acquisitions, and including the impacts of Metsera, I can attribute ~$3-4b as inorganic uplift by 2030. So now the question boils down to how much of the $6-7b can be expected from the pipeline - the biggest swing variable that will be watched.

That asking rate translates to a 4-5 major launches of $1b peak revenue potential and 4-5 smaller launches. There are 28 of 108 pipeline products in stage 3 trials. Sasanlimab is already in the registration phase. I am particularly hopeful about the obesity and diabetes space, which I think is Pfizer’s most visible growth option. Despite setbacks in the past, the success of an oral GLP-1 formulation could translate to huge upsides even if Pfizer can capture a modest market share. Around 10 launches over the next 5 years is a decent ask anyway, translating to ~10% of the overall pipeline and a third of the stage 3 trials alone.

Cautious Buy with a Watchlist

The dividends are an integral part of the Pfizer story given the limited valuation upside potential in the base case scenario. If revenues return to current levels due to organic and inorganic growth, there is bound to be a valuation rerating of 20-30% upside towards the historical norms of ~10x earnings in itself. Even ignoring that possibility, a maintained revenue level of ~$55b is good enough for a dividend yield of 2-3% on a cost basis.

The way I derive the dividend yield downside is to assume a ~25% operating margin and ~30% interest and taxes. For a $55b revenue, the cash flow (without accounting for capex and working capital) works out to be ~$9.5b, just about enough to account for the dividend payouts (5.7b shares outstanding *$0.42 dividend per quarter). Assuming 5-10% of revenue flowing into capex and working capital, I still see room for 2-3% dividend yields, down from the 6-7% levels today.

All in all, the thesis for Pfizer has very limited asks for a Buy to work out. It expects patent cliffs to be aggressive from day 1. It expects the growing products to not ramp up extraordinarily. It expects a ~10% conversion of a pipeline with significant stage 3 presence. It also assumes the worst case scenario for the dividends (no financial engineering or cost optimizations or working capital tweaks, etc.). Despite all the conservative assumptions, the current share price expectations are met, and a still credible dividend payout looks possible, in my view.

I would buy Pfizer at the current levels and keep a watch on how it negotiates the patent cliffs - any settlements or slower than expected revenue erosion could itself be valuation-supportive, and that is very much a likely scenario. Then I will also watch out for pipeline progress, of course, and acquisitions. I see decent chances of double digit total returns in Pfizer for patient investors - partially through the dividends and the rest through the decent optionality of valuation expansion back to recent historical averages.

data.title: Lam Research: A Semiconductor Sector Poster Child
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for US recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by, any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice, nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

Yes, We Are All Doomed. But Probably Not Today.

The semiconductor sector has seen its stocks and ETFs run up so far and so fast from the post-Liberation Day lows that it’s easy for the casual observer to decide, well, that’s it, all done here, time to take the money and run. And the casual observer may be right. Stock charts are extended, valuations are high, and at some point you would expect the sector to be a source of funds as large investors cash in their gains to then rotate to another less popular sector (software is a good candidate for that, but that’s a story for another day).

Before we get to Lam Research (NASDAQ:LRCX) itself, let’s just look at the sector overall. I like to use the ETF SOXX to do so - it’s fairly well balanced in its holdings of the top sector names and isn’t overweight NVDA in the way that the also-popular sector ETF SMH is.

Here’s how SOXX looks right now, starting from the April lows.

The factual parts of this chart are:

  • A textbook initial move up from the lows, quickly retraced for a higher low, then a big move up (that’s the Waves 1, 2, and 3; Wave 3 so far having reached the 3.618 Fibonacci extension of Wave 1 placed at the Wave 2 low, which is another way of saying That’s A Big Ole Run Up). Bullish.
  • Price has held over the 21-day exponential moving average solidly since early Q2, with only a few downside breaches all of which were quickly resolved to the upside. Note that on high stress days like 10 October, support held firm at that 21-day EMA (it’s the green line on the chart). Bullish.
  • Price cannot yet push up and over $295 and turn it into support. Potentially bearish, for if there are no buyers in size over $295, people will start to take gains, slowly at first and then more quickly.

The opinion parts of this chart are:

  • I personally like to project downside starting right away if a Wave 3 or a Wave 5 is at extended levels. Not because I think a stock or ETF is necessarily going to dump, but because I want to be prepared if it does. The Wave 4 here, which shows a potential drop to $243 (the .382 Fibonacci retracement of the Wave 3, a fairly shallow correction usually), is just that - psychological preparation. Until SOXX starts selling off, it isn’t selling off.
  • The final Wave 5 in this cycle assumes a correction which bottoms out at $243 and then puts in a typical final bull move up before a more substantial correction.

Now opinion being opinion, you can ignore all the opinion. Could be right, could be wrong, I have no more idea than you whether that’s how the SOXX plays out. It’s just one possibility.

But what you can’t ignore is price not breaking up and over $295, for that is a warning sign. If SOXX starts dumping so too with LRCX and everything else in semiconductor, yes including NVDA. So you need to know this if you hold semiconductor stocks or are thinking of doing so. Equally if price does move up and over $295 and turns it into support, there’s probably another leg up coming for all stocks in the sector.

SOXX $295. That’s your line in the sand. Sometimes securities analysis isn’t as difficult as everyone tells you it is.

Let’s take a look at Lam Research (LRCX) fundamentals.

LRCX Fundamentals

Remarkably strong is how I would describe the numbers here. Semiconductor continues to be the center of the universe as regards economic expansion, capex projects and geopolitics, and that is to the benefit of equipment providers like Lam Research. Unit growth in semiconductor, and in particular increasing function density and reducing feature size, are the unit volume drivers of Lam revenues; the company’s quasi-monopolistic market positions are the price (and therefore margin) drivers. Semiconductor capital equipment is a highly consolidated industry, and if you own the leaders in each of the major categories (LRCX, Applied Materials (AMAT), ASML (ASML), etc.) you can do very well during these cyclical upswings.

A generalized but ill-defined worry hanging over the semiconductor sector is “but what if the US and China decouple,” and I would say that this is a double-edged sword. Yes, there will be major disruption, but the No. 1 loser from that is likely TSMC (TSM); all US-based fabs will need to build out quickly and that means pricing power for companies like LRCX. Investing through such a dislocation in the world order will be challenging no matter what, but it will just require the usual discipline focused on price not narrative, volume not fundamentals. It doesn’t mean it’s all over for chips.

There's nothing not to like in those numbers. Revenue growth is strong, margins are high at the gross, EBITDA and cash flow levels; and the balance sheet with $2.2bn of net cash is safe as houses. The guide for fiscal Q2 (calendar Q4; the company’s financial year ends June) is for a drop in growth, but you can see from the last two December quarters that it's not a seasonally good month for growth. If the company hits the guide, TTM growth will remain at 26%, the highest level for some years.

Lam Research Valuation Analysis

Well, if you asked your grandparents, they would tell you that paying 28x trailing-twelve-month unlevered pretax free cash flow for anything at all is probably nuts. But this isn’t your grandparents’ market. In this market we’re in, the actual market in front of us as opposed to the market that might exist in Corporate Finance 101, this isn’t a bad price for the stock, in my view.

Lam Research Stock Chart

Let’s take a look at how LRCX itself is doing. It won’t surprise you to know that it looks a lot like SOXX.

With a stock that has run up this much - a 5.618 Wave 3 is a huge move - you have to have some caution. Another reason to have some concern is that the volume x price profile - that’s the gray horizontal bars on the chart showing volume of stock traded at any given price - is starting to pick up. I think that may be the beginning of institutional distribution, ie. profit taking. But we don’t have to guess. My rule of thumb for stocks like this is - watch the short-term moving averages. If LRCX is up and over the eight-day SMA (red line) and the 21-day EMA (green line), all good, nothing to worry about. A couple of daily closes below the eight-day but above the 21-day, have your antennae up but no need to freak out. Two daily closes below the 21-day, time to pack up and leave in my view. You can always get back in if it recaptures that moving average.

Rating

A cautious and disengaged investor would likely take profits here. But if you don’t mind watching price closely, I’d say this warrants a Hold rating until such time as there are two daily closes below that 21-day moving average, in which case I personally would take my money and run. You, as always, will do you.

Alex King, CEO, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 24 October 2025.

data.title: Snowflake: Strong Momentum And A Reasonable Valuation
data.description:
Snowflake's (NYSE:SNOW) stock has rebounded in recent months on the back of low expectations and a meaningful growth reacceleration. A lack of direct exposure to AI tailwinds and uncertainty around the impact of Apache Iceberg on Snowflake's storage revenue contributed to these low expectations.

I previously suggested that second-half expectations could be difficult to fulfill. Snowflake's stock is up close to 60% since then, though, with the demand environment seemingly solidifying and newer products supporting growth.

Snowflake's valuation is still reasonable for longer-term investors, although I would be cautious about extrapolating recent strength. As a result, I believe that Snowflake's stock will perform well over the longer term, albeit with periods of potentially elevated volatility.

Market Conditions

AI spending continues to dominate the IT market, with near-term growth expectations generally moving higher. The impact of this on software infrastructure companies has so far been fairly limited outside the cloud hyperscalers, though. AWS's recent growth could be considered disappointing given the AI backdrop. This is potentially problematic for Snowflake, as AWS still constitutes the majority of deployments. Azure is an emerging area of strength, though, with Snowflake's Azure business growing 40% YoY in the most recent quarter. This is interesting given that Microsoft (MSFT) has its own data platform (Fabric), which is targeted at analytics use cases. Fabric is tightly integrated into Microsoft's ecosystem and offers users a range of AI-powered tools. Fabric is likely to be viewed as a strategic area for Microsoft given the growing importance of AI agents. While the product serves over 21,000 paid customers, up 80% year over year, it is not clear that it is negatively impacting Snowflake.

The number of Snowflake job openings has fallen sharply in recent months, which could be suggestive of weaker demand, although it could also be due to seasonality and an increased focus on costs. Snowflake has also been aggressively hiring sales and marketing personnel in recent quarters, some of which relates to rebuilding the sales force after a period of extensive performance management in the prior year.

The number of job openings mentioning Snowflake in the job requirements has picked up sharply, which points towards improved demand. While this hasn't been reflected in improved net customer additions yet, Snowflake's revenue growth rate has already strengthened.

While it seems reasonable to expect software revenue growth to accelerate at some point, I would argue that Snowflake's direct exposure to AI is still fairly limited at the moment. Databricks has proven to be much more of an AI beneficiary so far, and recently raised to over a 100 billion valuation, up from a $62 billion valuation in late 2024. There remains a large opportunity that Snowflake is only just beginning to capitalize on, though. As a result, the company expects its growth to remain strong for many years to come.

Snowflake Business Updates

The adoption of open data formats is an important trend currently, due to the potential impact on Snowflake's revenue and its competitive positioning. Snowflake has seen strong adoption of open formats, with over 1,200 Snowflake accounts now using Iceberg.

Snowflake recently announced the planned acquisition of Crunchy Data for an estimated $250 million. Crunchy Data provides customers with tools to build Postgres solutions. Snowflake is now expected to introduce an enterprise-grade PostgreSQL database, which will help customers to run applications in Snowflake's AI Data Cloud. The integration of Crunchy is reportedly progressing well and is now being referred to as Snowflake Postgres. This solution is expected to be in preview in the next few months, and customer interest is reportedly strong.

Snowflake has also extended its connectivity platform with Snowflake OpenFlow. This service is built on the acquisition of Datavolo and makes it easier for customers to bring data into Snowflake's platform. OpenFlow expands Snowflake's business into the $17 billion data integration market. Snowpark Connect for Apache Spark is also now in public preview, enabling customers to bring Spark workloads directly into Snowflake.

Approximately 39% of Snowflake's customers had at least 1 stable data-sharing edge at the end of Q1, rising to 40% in the second quarter. There are also now 3,098 marketplace listings, up 21% YoY.

Snowflake's product innovation has accelerated in recent quarters, with a focus on:

  • Data warehousing
  • Data engineering
  • AI
  • Applications

Snowflake introduced 125 product capabilities in Q1, up more than 100% YoY, and roughly another 125 in Q2. AI is probably the most important area at the moment, even if for no other reason than it is where investors are focused.

Over 5,200 accounts were using Snowflake's AI capabilities on a weekly basis in Q1, a figure that rose to 6,100 in the second quarter. AI also reportedly influenced nearly 50% of new logo wins in Q2. While a number of the large AI companies are Snowflake customers, none of them contribute more than 1% of Snowflake's revenue.

Snowflake's AI offerings are fairly diverse, although they generally aim to either enable users or bring new workloads onto the platform:

  • Cortex AI is a managed set of AI and LLM features that make it easier to deploy GenAI workflows in Snowflake.
  • Cortex Analyst auto-generates queries, enabling business users to query structured data using natural language.
  • Cortex Search supports both semantic and keyword search.
  • Snowflake introduced Cortex Agent earlier in the year, which is a force multiplier for LLM capabilities. It provides orchestration and enables tool use.
  • Snowflake also recently introduced Cortex AI SQL, enabling customers to run AI models directly within Snowflake.
  • Snowflake also recently introduced an AI-powered migration tool, which enables customers to use Cortex to make the migration process more efficient.
  • Notebooks is a development environment where users can leverage SQL, Python, and markdown documentation. It is reportedly an area of strength at the moment.

Financial Analysis

Product revenue increased 28% YoY (excluding the impact of the leap year) in Q1 to $997 million, which was stable relative to the prior quarter. RPO increased 34%, while Snowflake's net revenue retention rate was 124%. Newer products are driving meaningful growth, with Snowpark and Dynamic Tables outperforming expectations in Q1. In terms of end markets, technology and retail were areas of strength in the quarter.

Product revenue totaled $1.09 billion in Q2, up 32% YoY, while RPO increased 33%. Migration of new workloads by some large customers drove outperformance in the quarter, and the Crunchy acquisition made a minor contribution. Professional services were also an area of strength in the quarter, although most of the professional services in the Snowflake ecosystem are being performed by GSIs.

Snowflake expects $1.125 to $1.13 billion product revenue in Q3, representing 25%-26% YoY growth. For the full year, Snowflake now expects $4.395 billion product revenue, representing an increase of 27% YoY. This guidance appears overly conservative given the supportive macro backdrop and the recent reacceleration of Snowflake's business.

Snowflake added 451 net new customers in the first quarter, up 19% YoY. Growth of Snowflake's customer base has been fairly stable in recent quarters, albeit at a lower rate than in the past. This is relatively disappointing given that Snowflake has increased the focus of its sales team on acquiring new customers.

With growth of the customer base stagnant, a sustained revenue growth reacceleration needs to come from expansion within existing customers. Snowflake's NRR doesn't point to this presently, but the way this metric is calculated means that it is highly lagging. Large customers are an area of strength currently, and Snowflake's NRR is likely to improve going forward. Snowflake had 2 large customers sign contracts worth in excess of $100 million in Q1, with both of these deals in the financial services vertical. Snowflake now has 654 $1 million-plus customers.

Snowflake's non-GAAP product gross margin was 75.7% in Q1, while its non-GAAP operating margin was 9% and its free cash flow margin was 20%. The company's non-GAAP product gross margin improved to 76.4% in the second quarter, and its non-GAAP operating margin was 11%.

CapEx has picked up in recent quarters, driven by facilities investments. This has had little impact on free cash flow though, which is largely being directed toward share repurchases at the moment. Snowflake didn't utilize its repurchase program in Q2 though, even though the company has $4.6 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and investments, and $1.5 billion of the repurchase program authorization remains.

Conclusion

While many software data infrastructures have seen their revenue growth accelerate in recent quarters, there are reasons to be cautious about over extrapolating the importance of this. Data is still somewhat mixed, and Snowflake benefited from FX tailwinds, the timing of Easter, and acquisitions in Q2. Shares are a "Hold" here.

Having said that, Snowflake's growth is strong given the current environment and the size of the company. I also think that Snowflake's growth acceleration is more genuine than some other companies, in large part due to the investments the company has made in product development over the past few years.

Snowflake's revenue multiple has moved significantly higher over the past 12-18 months, and has been the primary driver of the company's share price appreciation. If revenue growth continues to strengthen, I would not be surprised to see Snowflake's revenue multiple continue to move higher, although I would not count on this. Regardless, outside a shift in the demand environment, I believe that revenue growth should outpace any multiple compression over the longer term, resulting in solid returns for investors.

data.title: XEI: Tradeoffs For Steady Income
data.description:
I recently covered the Vanguard FTSE Canadian High Dividend Yield Index ETF (TSX:VDY:CA) here, and while I gave this dividend fund a buy rating, I recognized that it shouldn’t be a core holding and recommended a non dividend ETF instead to be the core Canadian exposure. Today, I will look at the iShares S&P/TSX Composite High Dividend Index ETF (XEI:CA), which is marketed as a “long-term foundational holding” by BlackRock: a potential core holding for your portfolio.

I will rate XEI a hold as I am not convinced of its potential as a core holding and while the fund is distributing high dividends, a comparison with its most direct peer VDY shows that the fund is actually less appealing.

Behind The Yield

The first feature that can make the iShares S&P/TSX Composite High Dividend Index ETF interesting is its high dividend yield. The fund distributes a high dividend of 4.95%, 1.39 percentage points above VDY’s 3.56%. The dividends are distributed monthly, which can be very interesting for an income investor looking for a consistent distribution. To do so, the fund is tracking the S&P/TSX Composite High Dividend Index. This index investment universe is the S&P/TSX Composite, and it selects 75 stocks based on their dividend yields and weights them with a Float-adjusted market cap method. This is a straightforward method to get high dividends, but it lacks a quality screening to ensure that the fund doesn’t buy yield traps.

This strategy comes with an expense ratio of 0.22%. It can be considered high for a core holding, but it is actually in line with the expense ratio of VDY, also 0.22%. This places XEI as a direct competitor and consistent with other Canadian income ETFs.

While yield and costs are very important for income ETFs, it is also interesting to see where the yield is made. The fund is heavily invested in two main sectors: financials (30.28%) and energy (28.74%). These two cyclical sectors are accounting for more than 60%, which is significant but consistent with the dividend approach as Canadian banks and energy producers are focused on capital redistribution. The rest of the fund is more defensive with sectors such as Utilities (13.78%), Communication (9.63%), and Real Estate (4.69%), balancing the cyclical exposure but to an extent that is only limited due to the small weights allocated to these sectors.

This is a focused income bet, a trade-off made to boost the income distribution but it also alters the risk profile of the fund by adding volatility and cyclicality. The high dividend is not a risk-free one and the fund is going to be dependent on market related issues such as commodity price volatility and credit market activity. The yield is attractive but it mostly relies on the health of two sectors, maybe not what you would want with a core holding in your portfolio.

I believe that while the yield is attractive today, its sustainability is closely tied to the fortunes of these two dominant Canadian industries, a factor that becomes critical when considering it for a long-term, core holding. You can see from the chart below that the dividend yield is actually very volatile over time, and currently at a high point.

A Slightly Better Diversification, But Not Convincing Enough

I recognize that the portfolio construction of XEI seems better suited as a core investment than the one of VDY, but only because VDY is already significantly concentrated. XEI, in appearance, would be better as an all-weather investment.

The fund’s portfolio is made of 75 holdings, which is a lot more than VDY’s 57 holdings and is actually a lot more diversified in terms of individual holdings. The top 10 concentration of XEI is 45.29%: significant as it is almost half of the portfolio, but considering that it was 67.74% for VDY, this is a significant improvement. The difference is even more important if you look at the top 3: 15.14% for VDY against 33.14% for VDY. This is linked to the index rule capping holdings at 5% and sectors at 30%. Without that rule, XEI would be significantly more concentrated too.

The lower concentration of the fund means that the fund is less dependent on shocks affecting single companies, which is a plus given that there is a significant overlap between the two funds. You will find in both cases holdings such as Toronto Dominion (TD) at 5.24% of the fund and Royal Bank of Canada (RY) at 5.00% that are also the top two companies of XEI. The only difference is the weights, and it is actually hazardous to draw a proper conclusion about them because the fund has a high portfolio turnover of 41%, surprising to me given that VDY was at 9%. The pursuit of the higher dividend comes with frequent rotation of the portfolio, and it makes the portfolio less predictable, another feature of a non-core fund.

Paying a Premium for Yield

The high distribution of the fund actually comes at the detriment of total returns, and while the concentration risk that was the issue with VDY is attenuated, the overall risk remains too high for what XEI gets you.

You can see from the above that XEI is a consistent underperformer, even when taking the distribution into account. Having income flows is high, but in the end if your total return is lower, the only excuse will be a significant reduction of risks. At first glance, you might think that this is the case because of the diversification of XEI over VDY, but the rest of this section will convince you otherwise.

Let’s first look at risk-adjusted returns with Sharpe ratios: 0.98 (3 years), 1.10 (5 years), and 0.68 (10 years) against 1.13 (3 years), 1.20 (5 years), and 0.81 (10 years). The Sharpe ratio of XEI is lower, because the lower performance of the fund doesn’t come with a reduction of the fund’s volatility (except 1% with 3Y returns), measured by the standard deviation: 11.30% (3 years), 13.40% (5 years), and 14.02% (10 years) against 12.14% (3 years), 13.78% (5 years), and 13.35% (10 years).

Of course, this is only a very limited measure of risks, as the volatility is not very representative of the risks of the fund and is not taking the concentration into account. I find the maximum drawdown to be a lot more revealing; over the last 5 years it was -13.86%: a more severe loss than the -13.17% of VDY.

Now if we turn to valuation metrics, it reveals that the approach of XEI is actually more aggressive than you would expect initially. The fund’s P/E ratio is 18.5 and its P/B ratio is 1.8, compared to 15.0 and 1.9 for VDY. The P/B is very close, but the difference lies in the P/E: To search for higher dividend yields, XEI takes riskier bets and pays more for them. I find it really hard to pay a 23% P/E premium to get higher distributions at the detriment of total returns and for a similar risk profile.

The market sentiment actually appears to agree with me as XEI is almost half as popular as VDY with AUM of CAD 2.5 Bn against CAD 4.8 Bn for VDY. The good thing is that the liquidity is enough; the average daily trading volume is CAD 4.4 Mn (0.17% of the fund), enough to support an average bid/ask spread of 0.05%. This is not as good as VDY’s 0.03%, but still very liquid.

Risks

The concentration remains a key risk for XEI. 60% of the fund is invested in two cyclical sectors, Financials (30.28%) and Energy (28.74%). For a defensive income portfolio aimed to be “a long-term foundational holding,” I don’t think this is very relevant. This makes XEI very sensitive to macro indicators like interest rates or commodity price. In addition, the rest of the portfolio invested in Utilities and Real estate also adds a pocket of sensitivity to interest rates.

The high portfolio turnover of the fund doesn’t really make sense to me with regard to the fund’s mandate and performance/risks compared to the competition. This is a risk because it decreases your visibility about the future of the fund, which is a problem for a long term investment. The lack of proper screening in the fund creates a yield trap risk where the fund buys companies with weak fundamentals but high dividends. I have not been convinced by the strategy of the fund.

XEI carries an inherent opportunity cost due to the low total performance given the risks taken. The benefit of diversification doesn’t really materialize as a risk reduction tool but as a performance reduction one.

Conclusion

I don’t think that the iShares S&P/TSX Composite High Dividend Index ETF should be considered as a core holding for your portfolio, not even as a buy when you would be better off with a combination of the Vanguard FTSE Canadian High Dividend Yield Index ETF for the income and the Vanguard FTSE Canada Index ETF for the diversification, as discussed in the analysis I linked at the beginning of this one. XEI is one of those ETFs where the high yield actually comes with trade-offs you should not be making.

data.title: September U.S. CPI: Soft CPI Keeps Fed On Course For Rate Cuts
data.description:
September’s inflation report delivered a familiar but mildly encouraging picture. Headline gains were lifted by volatile components, while underlying price pressures continued to ease. Core categories cooled further, hinting that tariff-related inflation may be proving short-lived, and services inflation, especially housing, continued its gradual slowdown. Overall, the data reinforce the view that price growth is normalizing, keeping the Fed comfortable with its current easing path.

CPI

US CPI comes in a bit below expectations in September, rising 0.3% MoM and 3.0% YoY, which is slightly below the expected gains of 0.4% MoM and 3.1% YoY. While the gains were below the consensus estimates, the annual rate was still higher than the 2.9% YoY rate recorded in August and the highest since the first month of this year. The volatile components have played a part in pushing the headline inflation rate higher since it dipped to a near-term low in April.

  • The general food index increased 0.2% MoM in September, a significantly cooler reading than the 0.5% MoM reading in August. The food at home subsegment increased 0.3% MoM, outpacing the 0.1% MoM increase in food away from home (which was that index's smallest gain since February 2024). Grocery prices continued to tick higher with 4/6 of the grocery subindexes rising in September, including cereal & bakery products up 0.7% MoM and meats, poultry, fish & eggs up 0.3% MoM. On an annual basis, food prices are up 3.1% YoY, and the food at home index is up 2.7% YoY.
  • The general energy index jumped 1.5% MoM in September, the largest monthly rate so far this year. The gain was driven by an increase in gasoline prices that was stronger than the seasonal norms. The not seasonally adjusted increase was 1.1% MoM, but after seasonal adjustment, it was 4.1% MoM. This was mostly caused by the unusual trend of soft gas price gains over the summer, which meant that there was no decline seen in September. Electricity (-0.5% MoM) and natural gas prices (-1.2% MoM) dampened the impact of the rise in gas prices. Importantly, the general energy index is now up 2.8% YoY, the highest since May 2024, which means that the segment has become an upward force on the headline annual rate relative to core inflation.

Core CPI

Core inflation also came in below expectations in September, printing at 0.2% MoM and 3.0% YoY, below the consensus estimates of 0.3% MoM and 3.1% YoY. This seems like the more important result of the report, as it suggests that price pressures from tariffs in September have come in below what was expected. The monthly increase of 0.2% MoM is a decline from the 0.3% MoM prints in July and August, and it led to a slight deceleration in the annual rate from 3.1% YoY in August. Both core goods and core services posted smaller monthly gains than the month before, supporting the narrative that tariff inflation is transitory.

  • Core goods prices increased 0.2% MoM in September, down from 0.3% MoM in August. Within the segment, there were mixed results among the subgroups. The softer areas, transport goods (0.0% MoM), medical care goods (-0.1% MoM), household goods (+0.2% MoM), and education & communication goods (-0.7% MoM), helped to offset the larger increases in apparel (+0.7% MoM), recreation goods (+0.4% MoM), and other goods (+0.8% MoM). The key used car (-0.4% MoM) and new vehicles (+0.2% MoM) indexes remained contained after looking a bit hotter in the last two months.
  • Some of the September data could be interpreted as showing some tariff-related inflation pressures. This is most evident in nondurable consumer goods segments like apparel (+0.7% MoM), sporting goods (+1.0% MoM), personal care products (+0.7% MoM), and pet supplies (+1.0% MoM). Additionally, even though the prices of cars and vehicles didn’t post a notable gain, the auto parts & equipment subindex increased 0.5% MoM and is up 3.1% YoY.
  • Core services prices increased 0.2% MoM in September, the lowest since May 2025, suggesting a significant cooling from what was seen over the summer. The shelter index was only up 0.2% MoM, and a softer print here is always going to keep the overall shelter gain contained. All other services subsegments came in with monthly gains between 0.1% MoM and 0.4% MoM, with the largest rise seen in recreation services. On an annual basis, the aggregated core services index was up 3.5% YoY, the smallest since November 2021.
  • As just mentioned, the shelter index was up just 0.2% MoM, as the index continues its gradual cooling. This lagging segment continues to post new lows in its annual rates. Specifically, the rent index was up 3.5% YoY, the lowest since October 2021, and the owners’ equivalent rent index was up 3.8% YoY, the lowest since December 2021.

In general, the inflation data in September told a story similar to the data that we saw over the summer. Core goods inflation, which was the first to decline following the COVID inflation spike, is contending with tariff-based inflationary pressures, while core services inflation, which has been stickier following the COVID inflation spike, is gradually declining as wage growth cools and consumers feel less secure in the current economy. Indeed, the commodities index (which does include energy) was up 1.9% YoY in September, the highest since April 2023, and the services index (also including energy) was up 3.6% YoY, the lowest since October 2021.

The downside surprises today were a result of that tariff-related impulse being weaker than expected in September, especially when compared to July and August. Although there are still pockets of upward price pressure, especially in consumer goods categories. That being said, the new tariff threats against China could create a more sustained increase in core goods inflation if they are realized.

Market Reaction

The long-awaited release of some official macro data prompted a significant response from financial markets. Investors reacted to the downside surprises with dovish moves across asset classes as they likely confirm that the Federal Reserve will continue to ease next week.

  • S&P futures (SPX) added to their early morning gains following the release of the CPI, reflecting anticipation that further easing is due in the two FOMC meetings of the year. Around two hours after the data dropped, the S&P 500 (SP500) is up around 0.8%, and the NASDAQ (COMP:IND) is holding around a 1.0% gain. As expected, the rate-sensitive Russell 2000 index (RTY) is the strongest gainer, up 1.4%.
  • In the bond market, traders were bullish in the short-term while the long end of the yield curve saw less love. The 2-year Treasury rate (US2Y) was down around -2 bps after two hours, and the 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) was basically unchanged. Both of these moves are higher than the initial drop in yields immediately after the release of the data (as seen in the chart above).
  • The U.S. dollar (DXY) saw a move similar to Treasury yields. Initially, investors sold the dollar off, but in the two hours that followed, the depreciation was reversed. Around two hours after the CPI release, the DXY index is flat or slightly higher, and the USD is slightly higher in key FX pairs like against JPY (USD:JPY), GBP (USD:GBP), and AUD (USD:AUD).

While this CPI report felt important because it came amidst a data blackout, the reality is that it was more or less an insignificant data point for policymakers. Communications from the Fed in the last meeting and the released FOMC minutes suggested that policymakers were largely subscribed to the “tariff inflation is transitory narrative," and it wouldn’t take a major upside surprise to shift them away from that. Also, the focus for the Fed is almost entirely on employment data, such that the CPI data feels like just a minor release. The market reaction reflects that as the major moves in rates and the USD were almost immediately reversed.

For now, it looks like the cuts in October and December are almost entirely priced in. The CME Group’s FedWatch tool has the cut probabilities for each meeting above 96%. Investors should continue to operate under these assumptions, especially with there being no labor market data to change the narrative.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Knight-Swift Transportation: Need More Evidence That Pricing And Volume Can Improve
data.description:
Investment Summary

My previous investment thought on Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings Inc. (NYSE:KNX) was a hold rating because the freight volumes were still weak, the margin in LTL was still soft, and TL pricing was not seeing any recovery. Recent results were better than expected, showing good momentum in LTL and cost discipline in TL. However, pricing weakness in TL, near-term margin risks in LTL, and weak freight demand temper the outlook. Management’s commentary around emerging capacity tightening and early bid-season stability is encouraging, but it’s not yet enough to confirm a full recovery.

Results Update

KNX Q3 2025 was mixed with positives and negatives. For a brief recap: Consolidated revenue grew 2.7% y/y to $1.93 billion, while revenue excluding fuel surcharges (xFSC) grew 2.4% y/y to $1.72 billion. By segments, Less-than-Truckload (LTL) continues to do well, with revenue xFSC up 21.5% y/y to $340.5 million; core Truckload (TL) remains under pressure, reporting a 2.1% y/y revenue decline to $1.08 billion; Logistics fell 2.2% y/y to $140.4 million; and Intermodal fell 8.4% y/y to $94.1 million. GAAP profitability was hurt by multiple one-off items like the $34.8 million in impairments, $11.2 million loss contingency, and $12 million in higher insurance costs at US Xpress. These pushed GAAP EBIT down 38.2% y/y to $50.3 million, with GAAP EPS at $0.05. Adjusting for these items, adj. EBIT grew 4.2% y/y to $106.0 million, and adj. EPS came in at $0.32.

Execution Improving, Positive Signals Emerging

Starting with the LTL segment, momentum continued to be stronger, which validated my previous view that the network investments would start paying off. Of the 21.5% revenue xFSC growth, ~14% of that was supported by an increase in daily shipments, which is as clear as it gets to indicate that LTL is winning market share gains, given the freight environment is still soft. This solid volume growth was coupled with an improved adjusted operating ratio (AOR) of 90.6% (250 bps sequential improvement). Further improvement in AOR seems achievable, particularly as KNX consolidates all brands under the AAA Cooper name, a move I believe makes sense strategically, as it simplifies operations.

As for TL, the cost-discipline theme from last quarter continued through to Q3. While the US Xpress claims did affect the segment’s overall AOR, there are catalysts in place that could drive up margins. There is an ongoing process to reduce fixed costs per mile. For instance, management is using a new way to manage the entire equipment lifecycle, starting from changing how they buy, utilize, and eventually sell tractors and trailers to minimize depreciation and maximize returns. And one of the ways is to improve asset utilization, which includes reducing the number of unseated trucks (basically trucks without drivers ready to operate them). By getting more miles out of each active truck and minimizing idle assets, KNX can better spread fixed equipment costs over more revenue-generating trucks. If these steps go well, we should see TL’s AOR come down to the low 90s from the current 96.2%, which is not impossible given that the legacy KNX fleets (excluding US Xpress) actually operated at a 93.7% AOR.

Perhaps the most important positive signal is from management’s comments. Specifically, they said that there is renewed regulatory enforcement around English-language proficiency and non-domiciled commercial driver’s licenses. My belief is these factors could meaningfully reduce capacity (the truck can still be there, but without a driver, it is not going to move), which would be very beneficial for KNX. They even called out that there are already early signs of tightening in select regions. The other early signal that a turnaround would come is that early bid-season discussions suggest less churn and even low single-digit price increases. More importantly, shippers are actively awarding volume to KNX. While still early, I think these signals represent the most reliable indicator that a cyclical inflection may be coming soon.

There Are Still Some Negative Points To Note

There are still headwinds. The most obvious one is the lack of real pricing power in the TL segment. While cost discipline and plans for better efficiency provide a buffer to margins, continuous pricing weakness means margins are unlikely to see any meaningful improvements. After a sequential decline in revenue per loaded mile (ex-fuel) in Q2, the metric only improved just 1.1% sequentially. My expectation is that pricing will stay weak for the foreseeable future because of a few reasons. The freight market is still in oversupply (despite the potential for easing soon), and that keeps competitive intensity high. Demand trends have also yet to normalize, and management even noted that while spot rates have stabilized from Q2 2025 lows, the partial recovery wasn’t enough to lift contract rates or revenue per mile. Until we see a more material contraction in capacity or improvement in freight volumes, I just don’t have a strong view that TL pricing growth will improve meaningfully.

A new concern of mine from the Q3 results is the LTL segment. While it did really well for Q3 2025, I see near-term risks, and I inferred this from management comments. They disclosed that demand through early October appeared “softer than the normal fourth-quarter seasonal slowdown,” and that led them to have a more conservative outlook for 4Q25, now expecting an AOR similar to the 94.5% seen in Q4 2024, implying a ~390 bps sequential deterioration from Q3 2025’s 90.6%. Mind you that LTL’s AOR was improving at a very solid cadence (94.2% in Q1, 93.1% in Q2, and 90.6% in Q3 2025, which is a much better trend than in FY 2024). With this reset to ~94.5%, I expect investors to take a more conservative view on AOR trends here, as what this shows is that LTL margins remain subject to volume fluctuations, particularly while the recent network expansion costs are still being absorbed. We could see wild swings in AOR until KNX ramps up utilization on the expanded network.

Valuation

Valuation has ticked up since July to ~24x forward PE, which is above the +1 standard deviation level over the past decade, inching closer to the +2 standard deviation level (27x). At this valuation, I think there is more downside than upside. There is still visible volume weakness in the near term that could really hurt the earnings growth. My sense is that, at 24x, the market is already pricing in a recovery over the near term, and that means expectations are running hot. For reference, the last time KNX traded anywhere near the +2 standard deviation level, multiples re-rated very quickly downwards in less than 12 months. I don’t know when the freight industry will turn around (it has been this way for quite a while), and I don’t think anyone has a crystal ball to figure this out. What I know is that going long at 25x means no margin of safety. Should things play out worse than expected (literally, it could be macro weakness that drives down freight activity), the stock sentiment could see a big downgrade.

Conclusion

I reiterate a hold rating on KNX. There is increasing proof of good execution, LTL momentum, and the potential capacity tightening, all of which point to a better medium-term outlook. That said, the near-term setup is mixed. TL pricing is not improving meaningfully, LTL faces short-term volume softness, and freight demand has yet to normalize. Valuation near 24x forward PE seems to already price in a recovery that may not happen anytime soon. Until pricing and volume improvements get clearer, I see limited upside from current levels.

data.title: Intel: Earnings Results Were Impressive, And Politics Remain The Play
data.description:
I figured Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) was a solid bet back in early August, believing that the Trump Administration would go out of its way to support the company. That instinct proved correct, and I’ve been bullish since, but I have to say the quick turnaround in earnings caught me a bit by surprise. In the three months ended September 27, Intel reported earnings of $13.65 billion, well above Wall Street’s expectations of $13.15 billion. EPS Normalized Actual trounced expectations, coming in at $0.23 versus expectations of $0.01. EPS GAAP Actual did even better, coming in at $0.90, $1.11 above expectations.

I went into earnings day expecting to walk away with a buy recommendation, but based more on future potential rather than current results. The solid earnings surprise, I believe, highlights early success in Intel’s turnaround efforts as well as demonstrating the tailwinds that government support can provide. While the stock may become a bit overheated in trading over the next few days, and I wouldn’t be surprised by a slight pullback, I believe that in the long run, INTC’s prospects remain bright and that there is still plenty of upside.

That said, the road ahead will likely be bumpy, and if the Trump Administration’s geopolitical calculations change, Intel could lose crucial tailwinds. Deals with Nvidia (NVDA) and SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) are also interesting, but it remains to be seen just how impactful they will be. Intel’s Foundry business is especially crucial from the viewpoint of the Trump administration and, thus, also investors.

Investors Need To Pay Close Attention To The Foundry Business

President Trump has made domestic manufacturing of chips a centerpiece of the administration’s strategic priorities. Taiwan currently leads the world in chip production, with the Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (commonly referred to as TSMC) (TSM) holding roughly 64% of market share. TSMC’s market share has been ticking up in recent years as well, even amid efforts globally to diversify. South Korea, and Samsung in particular, is also a leading semiconductor producer.

Before the deal with the White House, there was considerable speculation that Intel might exit the foundry business. In the third quarter of 2024, Intel’s foundry business lost a whopping $5.8 billion, and in the second quarter of this year, the operating loss weighed in at $3.2 billion. Aggressive cost-cutting initiatives helped INTC reduce the operating loss in the third quarter to $2.3 billion while generating $4.2 billion in revenue.

Currently, Intel’s foundry business is heavily reliant on demand from Intel itself. In the long run, the company aims to produce chips for other companies but so far has been struggling to find demand. Crucially, the deal with the Trump Administration will penalize Intel if it exits the foundry business within the next five years. Revamping the foundry business will be no small feat and will require excellent execution.

That said, it’s not all bad news. I recently wrote an analysis for Nvidia, noting how the company’s high margins would eventually be eroded by the basic “laws” of economics. One of the key points of this argument was that Nvidia’s high net income margins would encourage clients to diversify and, if nothing else, encourage NVDA to lower prices to head off potential competitors. I believe a similar process could happen with TSMC, which enjoys an impressive 43.29% net income margin that could draw envy. This could produce tailwinds for INTC.

Expect A Midterms Bump

The Trump Administration’s equity stakes in private companies represent a rather bold move, and I suspect the Administration will go well out of its way to support Intel and other investments. This could result in political pressure on companies such as Nvidia to encourage them to partner with Intel. Indeed, Nvidia recently announced a partnership with INTC and invested $5 billion in Intel. I suspect more deals will follow throughout 2026. These investments will probably be used frequently to highlight President Trump as a dealmaker and successful businessman and could result in a feedback loop generating even more deals.

INTC Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

Given all the negative press around the company, it may come as a surprise that Intel is actually on solid footing with resources comparable to both AMD (AMD) and Nvidia. Below you can see cash and equivalents top $10 billion. In total, the company has ~$30.935 billion in cash and short-term investments and a total of $51.731B in current assets versus $32.297B in current liabilities.

Intel really shines in the Price/Sales department (shown below). When analyzing unprofitable businesses, I often focus on P/S ratios rather than P/E ratios. Below, you can see that in regard to PS ratios, INTC is far below Nvidia and AMD. This makes sense given that Intel is not as greatly exposed to AI as the other two.

Speaking of PE ratios, if we annualize INTC’s latest earnings (23 cents) and then compute a PE ratio, we end up with a PE ratio of about 42.5. Of course, we can’t actually assume that Intel will maintain this EPS rate and thus need to be careful not to lean too heavily on this PE ratio. Still, compared to NVDA and AMD, INTC has a pretty low valuation. Yahoo! Finance currently reports that INTC has a PE of 88.08. Given that we are deep in a recovery story, this high valuation does not worry me and could improve throughout 2026.

Crucially, Intel’s effort to cut costs seems to be paying off rather quickly and substantially. R&D and MG&A (GAAP) weighed in at $4.4 billion from a year prior, a 20% cut. Net income (GAAP), meanwhile, is up 124% from a year prior. These numbers largely strike me as solid to good, and perhaps even great in some areas. It is nice to see early efforts producing tangible results.

Seeking Alpha analysts as a whole are rather divided, as shown above. The quant tools stop at a hold with growth getting an F, as shown below.

Why I Could Be Wrong

INTC is a pretty risky investment. The upside is sky-high but the company has fallen on hard times. Technological breakthroughs could lead to Intel falling further behind, and continued difficulties with chip fabrication may sink Intel’s foundry business. Quantum computing could make classical computing all but obsolete. And the economy could go into recession, dragging practically every company down. Many more risks lurk, but that's true for many companies.

Key Takeaway: Intel Is Still A Buy Even If It’s Not As Much Of A Bargain

I believe that INTC’s recovery is underway and could ultimately prove successful. I also believe that the fundamentals here are pretty solid, especially for a company that has fallen on hard times. Most importantly, for now, I believe 2026 could bring in more support and catalysts for INTC and other companies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: HCA Healthcare: Vital Signs And Technicals Solid, But Valuation Has Topped Out (Downgrade)
data.description:
HCA Healthcare, Inc. (NYSE:HCA), delivered strong Q3 earnings results. Shares jumped initially after the double-beat and raise, but some gains were given back by late in the morning on October 24. Still, the stock has been a clear winner over the past three years, within a broader Health Care sector that has struggled mightily compared to the S&P 500 (SP500).

I was bullish on HCA back in Q4 2022. Shares of the hospital company have returned 93% since then, actually beating the SP500 by more than 15 percentage points. The valuation is higher today, and I am downgrading the stock to a Hold from a Buy rating.

The Health Care sector appears to be putting in a relative trough to the SPX, while HCA’s leadership makes it a prime candidate for further gains in 2026, but the valuation is now full.

HCA Beat The S&P 500 And Health Care Sector Over The Last 3 Years

In October, HCA reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $6.84 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $6.32, while revenue of $18.6 billion, up 6.4% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $110 million beat. The company noted that same-facility admissions ticked up by 1.8%, while same-facility equivalent admissions rose 1.7% from Q3 2024.

Shares traded higher by 1% after the market opened following the earnings release, which was well within the 4.8% options straddle priced in ahead of the report. The stock may continue to swing around, as implied volatility hovered near 30% heading into the final week of October. From a bullish point of view, the stock rally in response to earnings was the first positive reaction in the past five reports, according to data from Option Research & Technology Services.

Looking closer at the quarter that was, it was yet another bottom-line beat driven by improved pricing and stronger margins. The top line was 3% above consensus, while the EBITDA margin beat by a firm 150 basis points. All told, EBITDA was verified 12% above what the street was thinking.

What’s more, the management team hiked its full-year sales outlook by a whopping $500 million, while boosting its EBITDA forecast by $450 million. If margins keep improving, that EBITDA could even be light, in my view. Comp sales volume improved slightly, but admissions and adjusted admissions accelerated compared to the previous quarter. Broadly, its pricing prowess was aided by Medicaid state-directed payment programs, so it’s questionable as to how durable that will be going forward.

On the earnings outlook, analysts expect very strong bottom line growth this year, with moderation in the out years. Revenue gains are less volatile, ranging from 4.3% to 6.3%. So, monitoring the EBITDA margin trend will be key.

As it stands, the sell side has turned quite bullish on HCA over the past 10 days, given a high 20 EPS upgrades compared to zero downgrades. With free cash flow per share now near $30, profitability trends look good.

HCA: Revenue & Earnings Outlooks, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, expectations should be held in check. HCA’s long-term average price-to-earnings ratio is just 14.3x. If we assume $28.55 FY 2026 EPS and apply a generous 16x P/E, then the fair value is near $457.

So, there just isn’t much left in the valuation tank right now. The stock also trades at a premium on a price-to-sales basis.

HCA: Valuation Metrics Reach Fair Levels

Key risks include a jump in labor costs, as that represents a significant part of the expense structure. Also, be on the lookout for any slowdown in volume growth (though current trends look sanguine).

There are unknowns around how upcoming Medicaid changes could affect the firm, though the management team has downplayed those risks throughout the year. Finally, competition is high in the industry.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a projected Q4 2025 earnings date of Friday, January 23, BMO. Shares trade ex a $0.72 quarterly dividend on Monday, December 15.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares close to fair value, the technical situation is rosy. Notice in the chart below that not only has the stock been a winner in a lagging sector, but a breakout has recently occurred. I was honestly on the fence about keeping a Buy rating, purely due to the technicals. We can project an upside measured move price target based on the consolidation from late 2024 through Q3—that's to near $550 based on the depth of the correction from $417 down to $290.

Also, take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has ranged in a bullish zone between 40 and 80. Also bullish is the long-term 200-day moving average, which is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under today’s level, there should be ample support on a pullback. $420 is support.

HCA: Bullish Breakout Targets $550, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a Hold rating on HCA. It has been a remarkable run for this Health Care winner, but I see shares near fair value across valuation metrics. The technicals, however, are very strong.

data.title: XME: Benefits And Risks Of A Metal Equity ETF
data.description:
Fast Facts

SPDR® S&P Metals and Mining ETF (NYSEARCA:XME) was launched on 6/19/2006 and tracks the S&P Metals & Mining Select Industry Index. XME has a portfolio of 32 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 0.47%, a trailing 12-month distribution rate of 0.32%, and an expense ratio of 0.35%. It is a large and very liquid ETF, with $3.3 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $230 million. The sponsor, State Street Corp. (STT), is a major financial services and bank holding company. Its asset management division, Global Advisors, has over $5 trillion in assets under management.

Underlying Index

As described by S&P Global:

The Metals and Mining Select Industry Index comprises stocks in the S&P Total Market Index that are classified in the GICS Aluminum, Coal & Consumable Fuels, Copper, Diversified Metals & Mining, Gold, Precious Metals & Mining, Silver and Steel sub-industries.

Additionally, eligible companies must meet requirements in liquidity and float-adjusted market capitalization. The index is reconstituted on a quarterly basis with weights based on float-adjusted market capitalization and average daily dollar trading volume, capped at a maximum of 4.5% in any constituent. The portfolio’s turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 43%.

The Metals and Mining industry represents about 19% of the market value of the GICS Materials sector, which will be used as a benchmark and represented by The Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLB).

Portfolio

XME has significant exposure in the steel sub-industry (33.8% of asset value) and in Coal & Consumable Fuels (21.1%). Other sub-industries are below 15%.

The portfolio composition is given as an example from 10/22/2025. Weights may have changed by the time you read this.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 49% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 6.9%. This will shift upon the next quarterly rebalancing, which serves to realign the holdings to the 4.5% weighting cap.

Among them, we find:

  • 3 coal mining companies
  • 2 precious metal mining companies
  • 2 steel products companies
  • 1 uranium products company
  • 1 aluminum products company
  • 1 rare earth products company

Fundamentals

XME has quality characteristics that reflect the materials sector. Its representation of various sub-industries can shift over time based on sub-sector performance as well as company fundamentals relative to the inclusion criteria.

XME's value characteristics within the materials sector reflect that it is cheaper than XLB based on valuation ratios and has inferior growth rates, as reported in the next table.

The next two charts plot the median earnings yield (a valuation metric, the inverse of P/E) and the median Return on Equity (a quality metric) of the metals and mining industry since 1/1/2000, based on my programmed data series in the Portfolio123 platform.

In summary, the industry is close to its historical baseline in both valuation and quality, based on 25-year averages. More precisely, it is slightly overvalued and marginally above the quality baseline.

Performance

XME has underperformed XLB by 2.5 percentage points in annualized return between 6/26/2006 and 10/22/2025, with a much higher risk based on maximum drawdown and standard deviation of monthly return (“volatility” in the table below).

This outperformance was mostly fueled by metal prices, in particular the strong rally in precious metals.

Gold and silver mining stocks followed the move, as plotted on the chart below:

The price action within sub-categories—like precious and rare earth metals that are exhibiting atypical upside at the time of writing—may shift significantly relative to the geopolitical, regulatory, macroeconomic, and interest rate environment.

Competing ETFs

The next table compares characteristics of XME and two non-specialized metals and mining ETFs:

  • iShares MSCI Global Metals & Mining Producers ETF (PICK)
  • Sprott Active Metals & Miners ETF (METL)

The former offers a broader geographical exposure, while the latter is actively managed based on company fundamentals and macroeconomic outlook.

METL is too recent to be meaningfully assessed. XME has significantly outperformed PICK and has higher trading volumes. It is more volatile but is still ahead of its peer in Sharpe ratio, a measure of risk-adjusted performance.

Who Should Consider XME?

XME offers indirect exposure to metal prices and is best suited for swing trading and tactical allocation in the metals and mining industry. However, it is uncompelling as a long-term investment, based on its historical performance and risk metrics.

  • Pros: offers exposure to metal prices, very liquid, beats its closest competitor.
  • Cons: long-term performance, volatility.

This article answers these three main questions about XME:

  1. Is XME meant for short-term trading or long-term investing?
  2. What are the risks to investing in XME?
  3. What type of investor is XME best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: UnitedHealth Vs. Centene: Next Week's Q3 Earnings Should Trigger Gains For Both
data.description:
Investment Overview

If we take a look at a graph charting the performance of the U.S. five largest health insurers by market cap - UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE:UNH), CVS Health (CVS), Cigna (CI), Elevance (ELV), Humana (HUM), and Centene Corporation (NYSE:CNC) - the difference in share price performance is remarkable.

CVS Health - up 43%, has performed very strongly. Humana and Elevance - up 14% and 15% - reasonably well, while UnitedHealth (UNH) - down 35%, and Centene - down 43% - the subjects of this post, are the stragglers.

This chart only tells half the story, however. In December 2024, CVS stock traded at $44 per share, down ~55% on a two-year basis, and as recently as June this year, Humana stock traded at $220, down >50% on an 18-month basis.

In short, most U.S. health insurers have suffered major downward share price corrections in the past couple of years - even Cigna stock sank to a low of $262 per share in July this year, down nearly 30% on a nine-month basis - but some have been able to stage remarkable comebacks, making this sector an intriguing one for the opportunistic investor.

Both CVS and Humana have suffered deep crises, missing earnings targets, withdrawing guidance, and, in CVS' case, firing numerous members of their senior management team and working with activist investors to try to return to growth. Clearly, these measures appear to be working - CVS stock is up >90% year-to-date, while Humana stock has gained a more modest 16%, but >30% since July.

My working thesis is therefore that, given UnitedHealth's business is very similar to CVS', and Centene's - being a pure-play insurer - similar to Humana's, and given that both companies stock price collapses occurred more recently than CVS/Humana, perhaps we should back both to stage a similar recovery?

If that thesis is correct, then now would be a good time to buy stock in either - I recently bought stock in UnitedHealth myself.

Why UnitedHealth & Centene Both Pulled Guidance Earlier This Year

The triggers for the selloffs in the health insurer market have been broadly similar for every insurer - an unexpectedly high rise in healthcare utilization among patients. Take the Medicare Advantage ("MA") market, for example.

According to KFF.org, an independent research organization, ~54% of eligible Medicare beneficiaries are enrolled in MA plans - ~34.1m people out of 62.8m eligible. That's up from ~19% in 2007. The share is forecast to increase to ~64% by 2034, and ~70% of all enrollees have independent plans, which are open for general enrollment. UnitedHealth has 29% of all MA enrollees - ~9.9m members, with Humana second, 17%, or 5.7m enrollees, and CVS third with 12%, or ~4.1m members. Centene has ~1m members.

According to Commonwealthfund.org:

The government pays Medicare Advantage plans a set rate per person, per year (around $12,000 in 2019, not including Part D–related expenses) under what’s known as a risk-based contract. That means that each plan agrees to assume the full risk of providing all care for that inclusive amount. This payment arrangement, called capitation, is also intended to provide plans with flexibility to innovate and improve the delivery of care.

Clearly, if the health insurer pays out less for the member's annual healthcare than it receives from the government, then it is able to make a profit - this is usually measured by the health insurer's medical benefit ratio - amount spent on healthcare, divided by premiums received.

Medicare Advantage offers unique advantages that traditional Medicare doesn't offer - eyeglasses, hearing aids, and dental care, for example, and most cover Part D prescription drug benefits, and in recent years, telehealth services. It can be restrictive in other ways, for example, members only have access to "in network" doctors, and prior authorization is required before some treatments of service are authorized.

Long story short, in its earlier days, MA plans were very lucrative for insurers, but in 2024, the Centers for Medicaid and Medicare Services ("CMS") opted to increase rate payments for MA plans for 2025 by just 3.7%, which insurers interpreted as an overall reduction, given rising healthcare utilization.

The decision had a devastating effect as companies with high exposure to MA saw their medical benefit ratios rise, and were forced to adjust, and ultimately scrap guidance. UnitedHealth suspended its 2025 guidance in May this year, and also announced the resignation of its CEO. Two months later, its stock price reached a five-year low price of ~$240 per share.

In Centene's case, the company withdrew its guidance on 1st July, no doubt in part contributing to UnitedHealth's ongoing declines. In Centene's case, it was its Affordable Care Act ("ACA") marketplace coverage that was causing problems, as well as its MA business, as analysts concluded that fewer people would receive Medicaid coverage, and those that did would require increased amounts of healthcare services.

In its Q2 earnings release, Centene noted that its:

Health benefits ratio (HBR) of 93.0% for the second quarter of 2025 represents an increase from 87.6% in the comparable period in 2024.

UnitedHealth noted on its Q2 earnings call that:

We now expect a full year medical care ratio of 89.25%, plus or minus 25 basis points. This compares to the initial 86.5% midpoint we offered at the end of last year, with the increase driven by the factors discussed.

Why Both Companies Q3 Earnings Should Trigger A Bull Run On Their Stock

Centene stock reached its lowest point of 2025 in August - ~$25 per share - as did UnitedHealth - ~$237. Based on current stock prices of $34.4 and $360.5, we may note a 35% rise in Centene's share price and a >50% increase in UnitedHealth's.

Buying the dip in August when uncertainty abounded would have been risky, but with the benefit of hindsight, it was also rewarding; however, both companies share prices remain heavily discounted to former highs, and there are reasons to be optimistic ahead of Q3 earnings.

UnitedHealth reinstated its guidance when announcing its Q2 earnings - revenues of $445bn - $448bn, and adjusted earnings per share of $16. The company has been fairly tight-lipped since, and I would not necessarily bet the farm on a guidance hike; however, I would be very surprised if guidance is downgraded, as that would seriously undermine the authority of the new management team, led by CEO Stephen Hemsley, a member of the Board of Directors, who was also CEO of the company between 2006 and 2017.

United's share price collapsed because the company got its sums wrong, anticipating higher payments than it ultimately received, with lower healthcare utilization, and the company has to show it has learned from its mistakes.

Nevertheless, there is good news for UnitedHealth in that the CMS has relented, and pledged to increase rate payments for MA plans by >5% in 2026. That won't help improve performance in 2025, but ought to give it a lift in 2026. Furthermore, UnitedHealth has announced that its individual MA footprint will be reduced by 109 counties in 2026, although it will still have massive MA exposure, remaining in 2,787 counties in 2026.

In Centene's case, the company recently reaffirmed its forecast for diluted earnings per share ("EPS") in 2025 of $1.75, and during a recent fireside chat at the Deutsche Bank Healthcare Summit, told the audience that:

Underneath that, the Medicaid results for July and August were supportive of the HBR (healthcare benefit ratio) improvement trajectory that we're looking for in the back half of the year. So pleased with that. We've obviously been very focused on the refiling process for 2026 Marketplace rates. As of today, we've been able to successfully refile in states covering 95% of our membership.

The key thing to note in relation to both Centene and UnitedHealth is that it is not revenues that are important, but profits. In 2024, Centene reported earnings per share ("EPS") of $6.33, a figure which looks set to decline by >70% in 2025, but I expect Q3 to be the signal that improvement is coming, and quickly. Management has advised that its MA division will not reach breakeven until 2027, but clearly, Medicaid margins are already on an uptrend.

In United's case, EPS - on an adjusted basis at least - will decline in 2025, but on its Q2 earnings call, CEO Hemsley told analysts:

Looking to 2026, at this distance, I would expect solid but moderate earnings growth. As we look further ahead, we see our earnings growth outlook strengthening quickly in 2027 and pacing steadily upward over the succeeding years.

Concluding Analysis: Both UnitedHealth & Centene Can Put An End To Crisis Talk With Positive Q3 Earnings - I Expect Nothing Less

Rising healthcare utilization did not just blindside health insurers in 2024 and 2025; it also blindsided the government, also, which is ultimately responsible for footing the bill for health insurance through the CMS and through Medicaid, and Medicare.

Arguably, however, it's been the health insurers that have borne the brunt of the backlash, as their profit margins - never especially high, despite the hundreds of billions of revenues taken in each year - have been squeezed, and their entire business models have been put under serious strain.

I believe the CMS' commitment to raising rate payments in 2026 for MA is a tacit acknowledgement that it, too, got its sums wrong in terms of how much it should pay insurers to administer healthcare, and going forward, I'd also expect insurers to ask plan members to pay a higher premium, in exchange for better quality of service. In terms of Medicaid, Centene's CFO had this to say during the company's recent fireside chat.

Medicaid is a tough business. It's hand-to-hand combat in terms of convincing your partner through data and what the run rate is, but feel confident that we're still on track to improve Medicaid going into 2026 from a margin standpoint off of our 2025 guidance.

UnitedHealth has ~400k employees, and Centene, ~60k. These companies must be incentivized by the possibility of making an annual profit, or their businesses collapse. The government and CMS is not being asked to fund a 2008 financial crisis-style bailout of the healthcare industry, but the industry does need to be stabilized and recalibrated, and I suspect there is agreement on all sides in that respect.

Because of this, I'm very doubtful that either Centene or UnitedHealth will lurch back into crisis on the back of Q3 earnings, and while I don't think either company will radically hike its full-year 2025 guidance when they report earnings next week, I do believe they will sound a more positive note on 2026 and beyond, and that, in turn, will allow them to complete the same kind of share price recovery we have seen with CVS Health, and to a lesser extent Humana, in 2025.

All things considered, then, my sentiment on both companies is bullish ahead of Q3 earnings (UNH reports pre-market on the 28th, and CNC pre-market on the 29th), and I would point to an additional positive. I expect both companies to report that the percentage of its MA plan members in plans rated four stars or higher (which qualify for additional bonuses) has increased, aiding profitability.

If I had to choose one to back over the other, I'd point to the dividends paid by UnitedHealth, which currently yields 2.45% per annum, adding additional insurance in the unlikely event of share price losses.
